evidence_quotes.quote: two clicks (checking the "I consent" box and checking "continue") is enough to make these practices legal.
And for the "free games" with copious amounts of microtransactions?
There are various e-industries which prey on vulnerability (minors)
misleading end-of-date cancelation of their services after paying full price
changes in the terms of service AFTER purchase

paragraphs.text: Thank you for opening this initiative for feedback. There are multiple online platforms that will take advantage of their customers financially by exploiting the current e-environment. I believe that the issues the Digital Fairness Act tackles could benefit consumer safety and protection. There are various e-industries which prey on vulnerability (minors) and a lack of regulation through misinformation, misleading advertisements (e.g. some games are advertised with features or content that are not included in the final product), misleading influencer endorsements, misleading marketing on their e-commerce sites, misleading pricing statements, aggressive monetisation tactics, misleading end-of-date cancelation of their services after paying full price, and changes in the terms of service AFTER purchase of the service. Services that are marketed towards minors or contain gambling-like transactions should be highly ethically questionable, especially when the service is hiding crucial information which would have prevented a purchase. People who want to escape gambling may trade one slot machine for a digital one without wanting to because these services hide these gambling "mechanics" (as some people in the industry would call it) on purpose to secure a sale. One of the more questionable practices is when fully paid services contain predatory in-service purchases that were not stated at the time of purchase. When a customer pays full price for a service, it becomes unfair when said service displays ads and microtransactions with gameplay essential features which were expected at purchase, not after. By exploiting the current lack of legislation and policies, these services capitalise by introducing more misleading "features" that are not aligned with the consumer. Another issue arises when, by not having a physical product, consumers may not be able to protect their digital purchases as they would a physical product. Consumers are led to believe that they own the products they paid for in full, when in reality, they do not. Some practices that have become more normal involve removing purchased products from customers' digital libraries without consent or reason, simply because a sentence buried within the user agreement allowed it to be. Some of these digital contracts only appear after a purchase has been made, and even then, two clicks (checking the "I consent" box and checking "continue") is enough to make these practices legal. These texts are often long and require an extensive understanding of legally binding terms, which the general public doesn't always comprehend. Despite understanding the words, it is not the same as grasping the legal meaning hidden behind the industry terms. I must also reiterate that most consumers are able to agree to digital contracts containing predatory clauses with as few as two clicks. These recurring practices have become worse with the current e-commerce climate, where more and more steps are taken to remove the consumers' right to own the products they purchased. Some services take advantage of this by launching what is marketed as a complete service, only to shortly after decide to shut it down when profits do not reach margins. It is a profit at the expense of the consumer. Vendors use these practices because there is no consumer protection against services that have already been purchased under the assumption that the publisher won't disable the digital purchase remotely in bad faith and make the service unusable before providing reasonable means for its continued use. And for the "free games" with copious amounts of microtransactions? Digital purchases are removed when the service shuts down, leaving the customer with nothing. Publishers have no obligation to disclose an end date because profits are higher when they don't. That is unfair. I expect to be allowed to use the service I paid for. If anything, publishers need to be transparent in how and when they will close the service.
